moves.trigger: Use when the deck needs an opening promise before any mechanism or proof detail appears.
moves.composition_rule: One focal native-shape or simple-SVG object must occupy at least 35 percent of the slide and anchor the product decision.
moves.typography_rule: Use one decisive headline plus no more than two subordinate labels; keep the support copy visibly smaller than the headline.
moves.ppt_primitive: native focal object with editable headline text
negative_rules: Do not use a dashboard grid on the cover.
Do not use an abstract promise without a concrete product-decision object.
Do not flatten the headline into a bitmap.
moves.qa_signal: The audience can name the product promise before reading any explanatory body text.

moves.trigger: Use when the story needs a selected problem or setup claim with a proof hook.
moves.composition_rule: Place one main claim in generous whitespace with one small proof hook and an object or divider that signals the next beat.
moves.typography_rule: Keep all claim text editable; build a clear size hierarchy between headline, proof hook, and labels.
moves.ppt_primitive: editable text hierarchy with native spacer shapes
negative_rules: Do not add multiple equal-weight claims.
Do not let whitespace become empty positioning without proof.
Do not repeat the cover scale unchanged.
moves.qa_signal: The slide reads as one chosen claim with a visible proof hook, not a sparse title card.

moves.trigger: Use when a repeatable system identity or mechanism object must carry the story across slides.
moves.composition_rule: A dominant editable object must occupy the primary visual field, change state across the sequence, and encode outcome or mechanism.
moves.typography_rule: Use labels only where they clarify the object's state change; keep labels secondary to the object.
moves.ppt_primitive: native shape or editable SVG system object
negative_rules: Do not use the object as decoration.
Do not replace evidence with a static emblem.
Do not overdesign the object with uneditable texture.
moves.qa_signal: The object visibly changes state and explains a mechanism or result without becoming a logo.

moves.trigger: Use when the audience must see why Run 2.0 data changes the deck relative to a weaker state.
moves.composition_rule: Show two named states and one highlighted delta; the implication line must be shorter than the state labels combined.
moves.typography_rule: Use editable PowerPoint text for state names, delta label, and implication; avoid table-like equal weighting.
moves.ppt_primitive: native split layout or editable comparison chart
negative_rules: Do not create a dashboard of metrics.
Do not add badges or rows that compete with the delta.
Do not leave the business implication implicit.
moves.qa_signal: A reviewer can identify both states and the single delta in under five seconds.

moves.trigger: Use when the skill mechanism needs to be shown as choices forming over time.
moves.composition_rule: Stage source data, compiled memory, generation, QA, and release gate as unequal weighted surfaces rather than equal process boxes.
moves.typography_rule: Use short editable labels on each stage; reduce explanatory text as the proof object becomes larger.
moves.ppt_primitive: editable staged diagram or shape sequence
negative_rules: Do not give every stage equal visual weight.
Do not turn the reveal into a report grid.
Do not explain all layers before the sequence appears.
moves.qa_signal: The viewer can see what changed from source cards to gated output before reading the notes.

moves.trigger: Use for the strongest outcome beat after setup, contrast, and proof have established the stakes.
moves.composition_rule: Break the prior grid and give the measured outcome with the largest justified delta the largest field, object, or chart area on the slide.
moves.typography_rule: Pair the dominant outcome object with one editable verdict line and sparse labels.
moves.ppt_primitive: editable big-number, chart, or dominant outcome object
negative_rules: Do not repeat the same density as the proof slide.
Do not make all comparison arms equal size.
Do not omit the before-and-after proof object.
moves.qa_signal: The slide feels like the deck's peak and makes the strongest measured delta visually largest without pre-claiming the winner.

moves.trigger: Use when the deck must close on governance, release status, and next action rather than more proof.
moves.composition_rule: Use a restrained gate layout with a small native SVG mark, one release status, and one approval or next-action line.
moves.typography_rule: Keep closing text editable and calm; support labels must stay secondary to the release gate.
moves.ppt_primitive: editable gate statement with small native SVG mark
negative_rules: Do not end with broad vision copy.
Do not add another dense proof panel.
Do not hide the public-release gate.
moves.qa_signal: The final slide leaves a clear approval gate and next action, not another argument.

moves.trigger: Use when proof detail threatens to crowd a main-story slide.
moves.composition_rule: Move dense evidence into editable appendix slides, speaker notes, or linked native tables while keeping the six-slide path low-density.
moves.typography_rule: Main-slide text stays short; detailed evidence text remains editable in notes or appendix material.
moves.ppt_primitive: appendix route, speaker notes, or linked native table
negative_rules: Do not compress appendix detail into smaller visible text.
Do not promote every proof note into the main body.
Do not break the six-slide rhythm to show audit detail.
moves.qa_signal: Main slides remain scan-friendly while audit detail is still findable and editable.
